refinement_sections.content: The primary objective is to develop a syntactic parser capable of accurately analyzing English sentences, focusing on the identification and labeling of various syntactic structures such as noun phrases, verb phrases, clauses, and their relationships. The parser should prioritize declarative sentences but must also effectively handle interrogative and imperative forms.

The parser will be designed to process a range of sentence structures, including simple, compound, and complex sentences. However, it will not address non-standard dialects or highly ambiguous sentences due to the inherent complexity and computational inefficiency they introduce. The parser will be limited to English language processing.

The design will incorporate contemporary linguistic theories such as Chomsky's generative grammar and dependency grammar. These frameworks will guide the parser's structure and functionality, ensuring a theoretically sound approach to syntactic analysis.

To evaluate the parser, a dataset will be curated from existing linguistic corpora, such as the Penn Treebank and the British National Corpus, focusing on a diverse range of sentence types. Additionally, synthetic sentences will be generated to test edge cases and specific syntactic phenomena.

The parser's performance will be assessed using precision, recall, and F1-score metrics, comparing the parser's output against a gold standard annotated dataset. Furthermore, user studies may be conducted to evaluate the parser's usability and accuracy in real-world applications.

A comprehensive report will accompany the software prototype, detailing the design choices, assumptions made during development, and the theoretical underpinnings of the parsing approach. This document will also include instructions for usage, examples of parsed sentences, and an overview of the evaluation process.
